title: A feel for the numbers
subject: Economics
objective: Estimate real-world economic magnitudes to within an order of magnitude
reflectionPrompt: Which number was furthest from your guess, and what threw your intuition off?

items.prompt: About how many people live in Ukraine? (millions, 2021)
items.explanation: Ukraine's population was roughly 41 million before 2022. Estimation trains a feel for scale, not exact recall.

items.prompt: Roughly how many countries are in the world?
items.explanation: There are 195 widely recognised sovereign states (193 UN members plus 2 observers).

items.prompt: About what share of an average household budget goes to food? (percent)
items.explanation: Food is often ~25–35% of spending; the poorer the household, the larger the share (Engel's law).

items.prompt: How many zeros are in one billion (short scale)?
items.explanation: 1 billion = 1,000,000,000 — nine zeros. Order-of-magnitude fluency starts with counting zeros.

items.prompt: Roughly how many hours per week is a standard full-time job?
items.explanation: A standard full-time week is about 40 hours in many countries.

items.prompt: About what percent annual inflation is often a central-bank target?
items.explanation: Many central banks target around 2% inflation — low, positive and predictable.

items.prompt: If a coffee costs 50 UAH and you buy one every school day, about how much per month? (UAH)
items.explanation: ≈20 school days × 50 UAH ≈ 1000 UAH — small daily costs compound.

items.prompt: Roughly what percent of the world's population uses the internet? (2023)
items.explanation: About two-thirds of humanity — ~65% — were online by 2023.

items.prompt: How many seconds are in an hour?
items.explanation: 60 × 60 = 3600. A quick sanity-check magnitude everyone should own.

items.prompt: About how many times larger is a million than a thousand?
items.explanation: A million is a thousand thousands — 1000× bigger. Confusing these is a classic scale error.
